Milestones in U.S. EPA and Environmental History
------------------------------------------------

![Image 4: EPA Seal](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2013-06/seal.gif)

A U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Timeline

![Image 5](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2013-06/1962_silentspring.jpg)

September 27, 1962
------------------

### Rachel Carson's Silent Spring Published

Rachel Carson's 'Silent Spring,' a critical look at pollution in the United States, jump-starts the environmental movement. Carson, a birdwatcher, discovered that heavy use of pesticides was killing off birds and making the forests 'silent.' She also wrote about harmful chemicals used for defoliation in the Vietnam War.

[Learn more about Rachel Carson](https://archive.epa.gov/epa/aboutepa/rachel-carson.html "link to archive")

June 29, 1969
-------------

### Fire on the Cuyahoga River

![Image 6](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2013-06/1969_cuyahoga.png)In 1969, the Cuyahoga River in Ohio becomes so polluted that it catches on fire. The fire helped spur an avalanche of water pollution control activities such as the Clean Water Act and the Great Lakes Water Quality Agreement. By bringing national attention to water pollution issues, the Cuyahoga River fire was one of the events that led to the creation of the federal Environmental Protection Agency and the Ohio Environmental Protection Agency. 

[Read more about the Cuyahoga River fire](https://www.epa.gov/great-lakes-aocs/cuyahoga-river-aoc)

![Image 7](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2013-06/1970_nixon.png)

1970
----

### President Nixon Signs NEPA

The bill forms the Council on Environmental Quality (CEQ) to advise the President on the environment and review federal agencies' Environmental Impact Statements, required for projects that would affect the environment. 

[Visit the NEPA Web site](https://www.epa.gov/nepa)

A Timeline of Major US Environmental Milestones
===============================================

Apex AP,Last Updated December 1, 2022

Have you ever wondered about how the environmental consulting industry took shape? Driven by a series of groundbreaking laws enacted by both state and federal agencies, and beginning in 1916 with the creation of the National Park Service, it is hard to imagine a time and place when and where our most precious natural resources were not protected with a mindset of preservation for future generations. Looking back, we realize how far we have come, paving the way for many other nations across the globe. We hope that this timeline puts our journey into perspective. We will continue to update it as new landmark laws are enacted. Thanks for visiting Apex Companies, and may we all seek to make the world a better place than we found it.

### Major US Environmental Milestones

![Image 3](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/plugins/cool-timeline-pro//assets/images/clt-compact-preloader.gif)

1916

![Image 5: Creation of National Park Service](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/uploads/timeline-nps-1.jpg)

Creation of National Park Service
---------------------------------

National Park Service Organic Act created the [National Park Service.](https://www.nps.gov/articles/quick-nps-history.htm#:~:text=On%20August%2025%2C%201916%2C%20President,those%20yet%20to%20be%20established.)

**Administration:** Woodrow Wilson

1947

Los Angeles Air Pollution Control District created
--------------------------------------------------

[First air pollution agency in the US.](https://www.aqmd.gov/home/research/publications/50-years-of-progress#:~:text=procedures%20or%20standards.-,On%20Oct.,been%20characterized%20by%20continual%20progress.)

**Administration:** Harry S. Truman

1948

Federal Water Pollution Control Act
-----------------------------------

[First major US law to address water pollution.](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/history-clean-water-act)

**Administration:** Harry S. Truman

1955

![Image 7: National Air Pollution Control Act](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/uploads/timeline-air-pollution-1.jpg)

National Air Pollution Control Act
----------------------------------

[First federal legislation involving air pollution.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/evolution-clean-air-act#:~:text=The%20Air%20Pollution%20Control%20Act%20of%201955%20was%20the%20first%20federal%20legislation%20involving%20air%20pollution.) This Act provided funds for federal research in air pollution.

**Administration:** Dwight D. Eisenhower

1959

California Motor Vehicle Pollution Control Board Established
------------------------------------------------------------

Created to test automobile emissions and set standards.

**Administration:** Dwight D. Eisenhower

1963

Clean Air Act
-------------

[First federal legislation regarding air pollution control.](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/evolution-clean-air-act) Amended in 1965, 1966, 1967, 1969, 1970, 1977, 1990.

**Administration:** John F. Kennedy

1964

Wilderness Act
--------------

[Established the National Wilderness Preservation System,](https://www.nps.gov/subjects/wilderness/law-and-policy.htm#:~:text=Federal%20Law%20Creates%20Wilderness%20Areas) a national network of more than 800 federally-designated wilderness areas.

**Administration:** Lyndon B. Johnson

1965

![Image 9: National Emissions Standards Act](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/uploads/timeline-national-emissions-1.jpg)

National Emissions Standards Act
--------------------------------

Represents an evolving federal framework within which [automobile pollution has been regulated.](https://www.encyclopedia.com/history/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/national-emissions-standards-act-1965)

**Administration:** Lyndon B. Johnson

1965

Motor Vehicle Air Pollution Control Act
---------------------------------------

Amended the 1963 Clean Air Act and [set the first federal vehicle emissions standards,](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clean_Air_Act_(United_States)) beginning with the 1968 models.

**Administration:** Lyndon B. Johnson

1965

![Image 11: Solid Waste Disposal Act](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/uploads/timeline-solid-waste-1.jpg)

Solid Waste Disposal Act
------------------------

Described by the EPA as [“the first federal effort to improve waste disposal technology.”](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solid_Waste_Disposal_Act_of_1965)

**Administration:** Lyndon B. Johnson

1967

California Air Resources Board(CARB) Established
------------------------------------------------

[CARB (or ARB)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California_Air_Resources_Board) is the “clean air agency” in the government of California. The board set emissions standards predating EPA.

**Administration:** Lyndon B. Johnson

1967

Air Quality Act
---------------

Amendment to the [Clean Air Act](https://www.epa.gov/clean-air-act-overview/evolution-clean-air-act). Enacted in order to expand federal government activities and also authorized expanded studies of [air pollutant emission inventories](https://apexcos.com/what-we-do/compliance-assurance/air-compliance-and-permitting/), ambient monitoring techniques, and control techniques.

**Administration:** Lyndon B. Johnson

1969

Federal Coal Mine Health and Safety Act
---------------------------------------

Generally referred to as the Coal Act, the most comprehensive and stringent Federal legislation governing the [mining industry](https://apexcos.com/sectors-we-serve/mineral-resources/) to date.

**Administration:** Lyndon B. Johnson

1969

![Image 13: National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA)](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/uploads/timeline-nepa-1.jpg)

National Environmental Policy Act(NEPA)
---------------------------------------

[One of the first laws](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/summary-national-environmental-policy-act) ever written that establishes the broad national framework for [protecting our environment](https://apexcos.com/what-we-do/environmental/natural-resources/#nepa).

**Administration:** Lyndon B. Johnson

1970

Reorganization Plan No. 3
-------------------------

Created the[Environmental Protection Agency(EPA)](https://archive.epa.gov/epa/aboutepa/reorganization-plan-no-3-1970.html) by Presidential Executive Order.

**Administration:** Richard M. Nixon

1970

Clean Air Act (Extension)
-------------------------

Major rewrite of CAA, setting National Ambient Air Quality Standards(NAAQS),[New Source Performance Standards(NSPS)](https://apexcos.com/what-we-do/infrastructure-services/landfill-engineering-waste-management/)Hazardous Air Pollutant standards, and auto emissions tailpipe standards.

**Administration:** Richard M. Nixon

1970

Creation of OSHA and NIOSH
--------------------------

The Williams-Steiger Occupational Safety and Health Act created[OSHA and NIOSH](https://www.cdc.gov/niosh/docs/2003-116/).

**Administration:** Richard M. Nixon

1970

![Image 15: Environmental Quality Improvement Act](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/uploads/timeline-eqia-1.jpg)

Environmental Quality Improvement Act
-------------------------------------

Passed to [work in conjunction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmental_Quality_Improvement_Act) with the National Environmental Policy Act of 1969 (NEPA).

**Administration:** Richard M. Nixon

1971

Lead-Based Paint Poisoning Prevention Act
-----------------------------------------

Law covering lead-based paint in Federally associated housing. Amended by Title X in 1992.

**Administration:** Richard M. Nixon

1972

Federal Water Pollution Control Amendments of 1972
--------------------------------------------------

Growing public awareness and concern for controlling water pollution led to [sweeping amendments in 1972](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/history-clean-water-act) of the Federal Water Pollution Control Act of 1948.

**Administration:** Richard M. Nixon

1972

Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA)
-----------------------------------------------------------

The [Federal Environmental Pesticide Control Act amended FIFRA](https://www.epa.gov/enforcement/federal-insecticide-fungicide-and-rodenticide-act-fifra-and-federal-facilities) in 1972. Also amended by Food Quality Protection Act of 1996.

**Administration:** Richard M. Nixon

1972

![Image 17: Marine Protection, Research, and Sanctuaries Act](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/uploads/timline-marine-1.jpg)

Marine Protection, Research, and Sanctuaries Act
------------------------------------------------

Also known as the [Ocean Dumping Act,](https://www.epa.gov/history/epa-history-marine-protection-research-and-sanctuaries-act-ocean-dumping-act) prohibits dumping into the ocean material that would unreasonably degrade or endanger human health or the marine environment.

**Administration:** Richard M. Nixon

1973

Endangered Species Act
----------------------

[Provides a program](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/summary-endangered-species-act) for the [conservation of threatened and endangered plants and animals](https://apexcos.com/what-we-do/environmental/natural-resources/) and the habitats in which they are found.

**Administration:** Richard M. Nixon

1974

![Image 19: Safe Drinking Water Act](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/uploads/timeline-safe-drinking-1.jpg)

Safe Drinking Water Act
-----------------------

Established to [protect the quality of drinking water](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/summary-safe-drinking-water-act) in the US. This law focuses on all waters actually or potentially designed for [drinking use](https://apexcos.com/what-we-do/water-resources/water-supply/), whether from above ground or underground sources.

**Administration:** Richard M. Nixon

1975

Hazardous Materials Transportation Act
--------------------------------------

Empowered the Secretary of Transportation to [designate as hazardous material](https://www.osha.gov/trucking-industry/transporting-hazardous-materials) any “particular quantity or form” of a material that “may pose an unreasonable risk to health and safety or property.”

**Administration:** Gerald R. Ford

1976

Resource Conservation and Recovery Act(RCRA)
--------------------------------------------

Commonly referred to as RCRA, our nation’s [primary law governing the disposal of solid and hazardous waste.](https://www.epa.gov/history/epa-history-resource-conservation-and-recovery-act)

**Administration:** Gerald R. Ford

1976

![Image 21: Toxic Substances Control Act (TSCA)](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/uploads/timeline-tsca-1.jpg)

Toxic Substances Control Act(TSCA)
----------------------------------

[Provides EPA with authority](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/summary-toxic-substances-control-act) to require reporting, record-keeping and testing requirements, and restrictions relating to chemical substances and/or mixtures.

**Administration:** Gerald R. Ford

1977

Clean Water Act
---------------

Amended [FWPCA of 1972.](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/history-clean-water-act)

**Administration:** Gerald R. Ford

1977

Surface Mining Control and Reclamation Act
------------------------------------------

[Prohibits surface coal mining](https://www.nps.gov/subjects/energyminerals/smcra.htm) within the boundaries of any unit of the National Park System.

**Administration:** Gerald R. Ford

1978

National Energy Conservation Policy Act
---------------------------------------

Purposes are to provide for the [regulation of interstate commerce,](https://www.congress.gov/bill/95th-congress/house-bill/5037) to reduce the growth in demand for energy, and to conserve non-renewable energy resources without inhibiting beneficial economic growth.

**Administration:** Jimmy Carter

1980

Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act(CERCLA)
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Otherwise known as [CERCLA or Superfund,](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/summary-comprehensive-environmental-response-compensation-and-liability-act) provides a Federal “Superfund” to [clean up uncontrolled or abandoned hazardous-waste sites](https://apexcos.com/what-we-do/environmental/remediation/) as well as accidents, spills, and other emergency releases of pollutants and contaminants into the environment.

**Administration:** Jimmy Carter

1980

![Image 23: Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/uploads/timeline-alaska-1.jpg)

Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act
-----------------------------------------------

Created most of the [national parklands in Alaska.](https://www.nps.gov/locations/alaska/anilca.htm)

**Administration:** Jimmy Carter

1980

Fish and Wildlife Conservation Act
----------------------------------

Enacted to [protect fish and wildlife](https://apexcos.com/what-we-do/environmental/natural-resources/) when federal actions result in the [control or modification of a natural stream or body of water.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fish_and_Wildlife_Coordination_Act)

**Administration:** Jimmy Carter

1982

Nuclear Waste Policy Act
------------------------

[Supports the use of deep geologic repositories](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/summary-nuclear-waste-policy-act) for the safe storage and/or disposal of radioactive waste.

**Administration:** Ronald Reagan

1986

Safe Drinking Water Act Amendments of 1986
------------------------------------------

These [first major amendments](https://sgp.fas.org/crs/misc/RL31243.pdf) were largely intended to increase the pace at which EPA regulated contaminants and to increase the protection of [groundwater sources of drinking water.](https://apexcos.com/what-we-do/water-resources/water-supply/)

**Administration:** Ronald Reagan

1986

![Image 25: Emergency Planning and Community Right-to-Know Act (EPCRA)](https://apexcos.com/wp-content/uploads/timeline-epcra-1.jpg)

Emergency Planning and Community Right-to-Know Act(EPCRA)
---------------------------------------------------------

Created to [help communities plan for chemical emergencies.](https://www.epa.gov/epcra) It also requires industry to report on the storage, use, and releases of hazardous substances to fe

Key policies and international agreements have been established for air and water quality, for plant and animal life, to heal the ozone layer, and to combat anthropogenic climate change. At the same time, a number of major environmental disasters—some with global reach—have left their imprint on our Earth and on our collective consciousness. # A Timeline of Environmental History

Nearly all of the major milestones in U.S. and global environmental legislation have been enacted since the 1960s. Key [policies](https://www.britannica.com/topic/environmental-policy) and international agreements have been established for air and water quality, for plant and animal life, to heal the [ozone layer](https://www.britannica.com/science/ozone-layer), and to combat anthropogenic [climate change](https://www.britannica.com/science/global-warming). At the same time, a number of major environmental disasters—some with global reach—have left their imprint on our Earth and on our collective consciousness. Learn about the international efforts and [grassroots activism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/environmentalism) that have changed some of the ways that we interact with our precious planet for the better, and take an honest look at just how far human stewardship still needs to advance.

Celebrating Pioneers In Environmental Law
-----------------------------------------

Nearly 50 years ago, thousands of Americans rallied in the spring of 1970 to celebrate the first Earth Day, an event that marked the beginning of the modern environmental movement. As we approach the 50-year milestone, the Environmental Law Institute has interviewed and recorded the stories of 24 of the men and women who inspired, created, and implemented U.S. framework laws to protect public health and the environment. We asked them why they chose to work on environmental problems and what caused the rise in public concern and support for new environmental laws in the early days. We learned how sweeping new laws like the Clean Air Act and the Clean Water Act came to be created and carried out by able leaders from both political parties, what innovations in those laws made environmental law work well, and where they fell short of expectations.

The pioneers also provided perspective on today’s environmental agenda and how the lessons of the past might help today’s leaders adopt new approaches to move it forward. We were told that today’s successful strategies are likely to be more collaborative and decentralized, better informed by science and communications technology, and led by a new generation of leaders in all sectors, undaunted by the complexity and global scale of today’s environmental risks. But we also heard that bold action on a new national environmental agenda may be out of reach with the growing gulf between the Democratic and Republican parties on what should be done to tackle 21st century problems and the inability of congressional leaders to forge bipartisan solutions. We believe our viewers and listeners will be intrigued and informed by many fascinating personal portraits of these pioneers and their reflections on what it will take to move forward.

Scott Fulton

 President, Environmental Law Institute

enriched_points.expanded_content: Environmental law has evolved significantly since the early 20th century, addressing growing concerns over pollution and resource conservation through various legislative acts and regulations.
Key milestones include the establishment of the EPA in 1970, the Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, and the National Environmental Policy Act, shaping environmental protection in the U.S.
Historical environmental laws and milestones have informed today's regulatory frameworks, guiding modern policy-making to address climate change, pollution control, and sustainable development.
